sections.text: AP Calculus AB. Module One — limits and continuity. This is where calculus actually starts. Two ideas, both kind of weird the first time you see them, but everything else this year is built on top.

Imagine you're walking toward a wall, and every step you take, you only go halfway there. Halfway, then halfway of what's left, then halfway of that. You'd be walking forever and you'd never actually touch the wall. But you'd get arbitrarily close. That gap shrinks toward zero. That — right there — is what a limit is. It's not 'the value at the wall.' It's the value you're approaching, even if you never land on it.

Three things today. One — what a limit is, and the notation for it. Two — one-sided limits, and when a limit doesn't exist. Three — what continuity actually means, with the three-condition test. By the end you should be able to look at a graph and tell me both the limit at any point and whether the function is continuous there.

A limit asks: as x gets really, really close to some value — call it a — what value is f of x heading toward? Notice what we're not asking. We're not asking what f of a equals. We're asking what f of x approaches as x sneaks up on a. Sometimes those are the same number. Sometimes they're different. That's why limits are their own thing.

Here's the notation. Lim, with x arrow a written underneath, then f of x — equals L. You read that as: the limit, as x approaches a, of f of x, equals L. The little arrow is doing a lot of work. It says x is getting closer and closer to a, but it never has to actually be a. Get used to that arrow. You'll see it for the rest of this course.

Now — x can approach a from two directions. From the left, meaning slightly smaller numbers creeping up. Or from the right, meaning slightly larger numbers creeping down. We write left-side with a tiny minus sign on the a, right-side with a tiny plus. If both sides agree on the same value, then the regular two-sided limit exists and equals that value. If left and right disagree, the two-sided limit does not exist.

When does a limit fail to exist? Three classic ways. One — left and right approach different values. The function jumps. Two — the function blows up to infinity, no single number it's heading toward. Three — the function oscillates forever near a, never settling, like sine of one over x near zero. In all three cases we say the limit DNE — does not exist. That's actually a perfectly valid answer on the AP exam.

Your turn. Look at this graph. As x approaches two from the left, the function heads toward three. As x approaches two from the right, the function heads toward five. Question: what is the two-sided limit at x equals two? Pause the video, decide, come back.

Welcome back. Left side gives three. Right side gives five. They don't agree. So the two-sided limit at x equals two does not exist. The left-hand limit is three, the right-hand limit is five — those each exist on their own — but the regular limit needs them to match, and they don't. DNE is the right answer.

Now — continuity. A function is continuous at x equals a if you can draw it through that point without lifting your pencil. Formally, three things have to be true. One — f of a actually exists. The function has a value there. Two — the limit as x approaches a exists. Both sides agree. Three — and this is the kicker — those two numbers are equal. f of a equals the limit. All three. If even one fails, f is discontinuous at a.

And there are three flavors of discontinuity you should be able to name. Removable — there's a hole in the graph, like the function is otherwise fine but missing one point. Jump — the graph leaps from one value to another, classic step function. Infinite — the function shoots off to plus or minus infinity, vertical asymptote. AP loves to ask: classify the discontinuity. Memorize those three names.

One real-world tie-in before we wrap. Speed at a single instant — how fast you're going right now, not over the last second — that's a limit. Average speed over an interval, then squeeze the interval down to zero width. The number you approach is your instantaneous speed. That's literally what your speedometer shows. We'll formalize that in Module Three when we define the derivative. But the engine underneath is — yep — a limit.

Recap. A limit is the value f of x approaches as x approaches a — not the value at a. Notation is lim, x arrow a, f of x. Two-sided limits need left and right to agree. A limit can fail to exist three ways: jump, infinity, oscillation. Continuity needs three conditions: f of a exists, the limit exists, and they're equal. Three flavors of discontinuity: removable, jump, infinite.

Important — this video is the overview, not the whole module. To actually master limits and continuity, here's the path. One — read OpenStax Calculus Volume One, Chapter Two. The graphical limit examples are gold. Two — go to Khan Academy AP Calc AB Unit One and work through the twenty problems on estimating limits from graphs and tables. Three — do the assignment in your dashboard: a single function, evaluated three ways. Four — when you're ready, take the AP Classroom Personal Progress Check. If anything feels fuzzy, message your advisor. See you in Module Two — limit laws and the squeeze theorem.
